content:
Multivariable calculus in the 19th century—through **Lagrange**, **Cauchy** and **Hamilton**—extended single-variable derivatives to **partial derivatives** and vector differential operators including **grad** (∇).

The gradient emerged as the natural object for **potential fields** in physics: force as minus the gradient of potential energy. **Gibbs** and **Heaviside** popularized vector calculus notation (∇, div, curl) that engineers still use today.

In the 20th century, **gradient methods** for optimization (steepest descent, later momentum and quasi-Newton ideas) connected ∇f directly to algorithms. **Automatic differentiation** (1960s onward, e.g. Wengert, later backprop in neural networks) made exact gradients of composite programs practical at scale.

Modern deep learning treats ∇L as the central signal: backpropagation is the chain rule applied systematically to compute gradient vectors for every trainable parameter in billion-weight models.

timeline.label: Vector calculus formalism
timeline.description: ∇f defined via partial derivatives; geometric link to level sets and steepest ascent established.

timeline.label: Gradient-based optimization
timeline.description: Steepest descent and variants use −∇f to minimize objectives numerically.

timeline.label: Backpropagation in neural nets
timeline.description: Rumelhart, Hinton and Williams (1986) popularized efficient gradient computation through layered networks.

timeline.label: Autograd at scale
timeline.description: Frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch, JAX) compute ∇L for massive graphs on accelerators—gradients drive everyday Gen AI training.
